title: CLAUDE.md
text: package centers two workflows: experimental design generation post-model analysis/visualisation. Exported functions listed NAMESPACE (auto-generated — hand-edit).

title: CLAUDE.md
text: R/design.R → design() main entry point. dispatches type (crd, rcbd, lsd, split, strip, crossed:<base>) underlying agricolae design generators, enriches result. - R/design_helpers.R — internal helpers (des_info, get_design_info, etc.) augment raw agricolae design object row/column layout. - R/satab.R — builds skeletal ANOVA table (satab object, S3 class print method) describing design’s sources variation. - R/create_buffers.R (add_buffers) — adds buffer plots (edge/rows/columns/blocks) design layout. - R/export_excel.R (export_design_to_excel) — writes design styled workbook. - design() returns list class design containing $design (data frame), $plot.des (ggplot/patchwork), $satab, optionally $seed. Plotting done via autoplot.design S3 method.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: functions operate already-fitted model objects (aov, asreml, lme/nlme, lmerMod/lme4, sommer, ARTool). package model-engine-agnostic via S3 dispatch Enhances/Suggests packages. - R/mct.R (multiple_comparisons) — Tukey HSD / multiple comparison predicted means, letter groupings (multcompView), back-transformation support, confidence/comparison intervals. Returns mct S3 object (print autoplot methods). - R/prediction_methods.R — get_predictions() generics extract predicted means supported model class; abstraction layer lets multiple_comparisons work across engines. - R/resplot.R + R/resplot_methods.R (resplot/resplt) — diagnostic residual plots, dispatched per model class. - R/heatmap.R (heat_map) — spatial heat maps trial data/residuals row/column. - R/variogram.r (variogram) — spatial variograms ASReml-R models (see two reference PDFs repo root: Stefanova 2009, Gilmour 1997). - R/summary_graph.R (summary_graph), R/logltest.R (logl_test) — additional analysis helpers. - R/autoplot.R — S3 autoplot methods (autoplot.design, autoplot.mct) built ggplot2; patchwork composes multi-panel output.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Uses testthat edition 3 parallel execution enabled (Config/testthat/edition: 3, Config/testthat/parallel: true). Visual/plot regression tests rely snapshot SVGs tests/testthat/_snaps/. Snapshots split ggplot2 version (ggplot2-new vs ggplot2-old) — see ggplot2_variant() tests/testthat/helper-expectations.R. NEVER accept failing snapshot without user reviewing first. snapshot test fails output changed; blindly running snapshot_accept() (accepting bulk) makes test pass potentially baking broken output — defeating entire point test. required workflow snapshot fails: (1) show user changed (e.g. testthat::snapshot_review(), diff .new.svg committed .svg), (2) explain believe change correct, (3) let user confirm, accept. user must review snapshot changes — decision make behalf, even diff looks minor plausible explanation. run snapshot_accept() unprompted. tests/testthat/helper-expectations.R defines custom expectations (e.g. expect_design_output, equivalent_ggplot2) helpers — reuse rather re-asserting structure inline. tests/testthat/data/*.Rdata pre-fitted model objects supported engine, tests don’t need (commercial/heavy) modelling packages installed test time. setup-*.R / teardown-*.R files manage per-suite fixtures (e.g. teardown-install_asreml.R).

title: Choosing and interpreting multiple comparisons
text: fitting model, common next step compare predicted treatment means. biometryassist provides three complementary functions , one want depends comparing: multiple_comparisons() means-centric. returns one row per treatment level (mean, standard error, confidence interval) summarises complete set pairwise comparisons compact letter display (treatments share letter show evidence difference). pairwise_comparisons() difference-centric. returns tidy table one row per requested comparison, honest representation care comparisons, general contrasts. reference_comparisons() means-centric, specific question “treatment compare single control?”. reports level’s mean, reference mean adjusted difference, using exact Dunnett test default. “Means-centric” “difference-centric” describe function presents results, tests. three rest underlying pairwise differences predicted means (generally, linear contrasts): multiple_comparisons() tests every pairwise difference condenses letters means (test means ), whereas pairwise_comparisons() reference_comparisons() report chosen subset differences directly. rest vignette tours three functions shared example data, covers cross-cutting topics: choosing multiplicity adjustment, shared adjust arguments, confidence intervals, summary guide picking right function. examples use built-data aov() run without specialised modelling packages, three functions work across supported model types (aov, lm, aovlist, asreml, lmerMod lme).

title: Choosing and interpreting multiple comparisons
text: start iris data one-way model petal width, reuse across first three subsections analysis can seen function’s perspective. multiple_comparisons() compares every pair means summarises result letter display means, using Tukey’s HSD default: row treatment mean; treatments share letter show evidence difference. autoplot() draws means intervals letters:  letter display compact summary pairwise comparisons . every pair compared, “share letter” means “evidence difference” — interpretation valid complete set (see model, several views). pairwise_comparisons() reports comparisons , one per row. default tests pairwise differences adjusts p-values Holm’s method: row difference: estimate mean(level1) - mean(level2), level1/level2 making direction explicit level1.mean / level2.mean columns reporting two predicted means computed . care specific set comparisons, pass via pairs — multiplicity adjustment applied just set: autoplot() draws forest plot differences, dashed line zero asterisk marking comparisons significant (adjusted) level:  Sometimes comparison interest isn’t single pair. contrasts argument tests linear contrast — example, setosa versus average two species: contrast named vector coefficients (sum zero) list name becomes row label. two-level contrast c(= 1, B = -1) exactly pairwise difference "-B". question specifically “treatment compare single control?”, reference_comparisons() natural tool. means-centric like multiple_comparisons(), comparisons chosen reference made — every comparison shares reference, significance attaches cleanly treatment (letter display incomplete set; see model, several views). Switching chickwts data (chick weights feed type), compare every feed casein control: row gives mean feed (level1.mean), mean control (level2.mean) difference, adjustment defaulting exact Dunnett test. autoplot() method draws feed’s mean, dashed line control mean (marked diamond), interval difference control, interval clears control line exactly comparison significant; significant feeds also flagged asterisk:  three functions different views fitted model, different tests. iris model, multiple_comparisons() -pairs pairwise_comparisons() rest identical pairwise differences — one presents means--letters, table differences. correct; answer different questions: letter display (multiple_comparisons()) compact summary complete set pairwise comparisons, valid every pair compared; difference table (pairwise_comparisons()) honest representation set incomplete irregular — forcing letters onto set silently assert “different” pairs never tested; reference table (reference_comparisons()) right view one level control, significance attaches treatment directly. choice function follows question, model.
code: model <- aov(Petal.Width ~ Species, data = iris) multiple_comparisons(model, classify = "Species") ## Multiple Comparisons of Means: Tukey's HSD Test ## Significance level: 0.05  ## HSD value: 0.0969097  ##  ## Predicted values: ##      Species predicted.value std.error  df groups   ci  low   up ## 1     setosa            0.25      0.03 147      a 0.06 0.19 0.30 ## 2 versicolor            1.33      0.03 147      b 0.06 1.27 1.38 ## 3  virginica            2.03      0.03 147      c 0.06 1.97 2.08 autoplot(multiple_comparisons(model, classify = "Species"), label_height = 0.95) pairwise_comparisons(model, classify = "Species") ## Pairwise comparisons of means ## Classify: Species  ## Adjustment method: holm  ## Significance level: 0.05  ##  ##       level1     level2             comparison estimate level1.mean level2.mean ## 1     setosa versicolor    setosa - versicolor    -1.08        0.25        1.33 ## 2     setosa  virginica     setosa - virginica    -1.78        0.25        2.03 ## 3 versicolor  virginica versicolor - virginica    -0.70        1.33        2.03 ##   std.error statistic  df  p.value conf.low conf.high ## 1      0.04    -26.39 147 2.51e-57    -1.16     -1.00 ## 2      0.04    -43.49 147 2.39e-85    -1.86     -1.70 ## 3      0.04    -17.10 147 8.82e-37    -0.78     -0.62 pairwise_comparisons(   model,   classify = "Species",   pairs = c("setosa-versicolor", "versicolor-virginica") ) ## Pairwise comparisons of means ## Classify: Species  ## Adjustment method: holm  ## Significance level: 0.05  ##  ##       level1     level2             comparison estimate level1.mean level2.mean ## 1     setosa versicolor    setosa - versicolor    -1.08        0.25        1.33 ## 2 versicolor  virginica versicolor - virginica    -0.70        1.33        2.03 ##   std.error statistic  df  p.value conf.low conf.high ## 1      0.04    -26.39 147 2.51e-57    -1.16     -1.00 ## 2      0.04    -17.10 147 8.82e-37    -0.78     -0.62 autoplot(pairwise_comparisons(model, classify = "Species")) pairwise_comparisons(   model,   classify = "Species",   contrasts = list(     "setosa vs rest" = c(setosa = 1, versicolor = -0.5, virginica = -0.5)   ) ) ## Contrasts of means ## Classify: Species  ## Adjustment method: holm  ## Significance level: 0.05  ##  ##       comparison estimate std.error statistic  df  p.value conf.low conf.high ## 1 setosa vs rest    -1.43      0.04    -40.34 147 2.12e-81     -1.5     -1.36 model_cw <- aov(weight ~ feed, data = chickwts) reference_comparisons(model_cw, classify = "feed", reference = "casein") ## Comparisons against a reference level ## Classify: feed  ## Reference: casein  ## Adjustment method: dunnett  ## Significance level: 0.05  ##  ##      level1 level2         comparison estimate level1.mean level2.mean ## 1 horsebean casein horsebean - casein  -163.38      160.20      323.58 ## 2   linseed casein   linseed - casein  -104.83      218.75      323.58 ## 3  meatmeal casein  meatmeal - casein   -46.67      276.91      323.58 ## 4   soybean casein   soybean - casein   -77.15      246.43      323.58 ## 5 sunflower casein sunflower - casein     5.33      328.92      323.58 ##   std.error statistic df  p.value conf.low conf.high ## 1     23.49     -6.96 65 5.65e-09  -223.89   -102.88 ## 2     22.39     -4.68 65 7.84e-05  -162.53    -47.14 ## 3     22.90     -2.04 65 1.67e-01  -105.66     12.31 ## 4     21.58     -3.58 65 3.11e-03  -132.75    -21.56 ## 5     22.39      0.24 65 9.99e-01   -52.36     63.03 autoplot(reference_comparisons(model_cw, classify = "feed", reference = "casein"))

title: Choosing and interpreting multiple comparisons
text: Testing several comparisons inflates chance least one false positive, p-values adjusted. two broad families error rate, right choice depends goal. Family-wise error rate (FWER) probability making false rejection across whole family. conservative, “every claim must trustworthy” criterion, appropriate small set planned comparisons. Bonferroni simplest FWER method, quite conservative: Holm controls error rate uniformly powerful, essentially never reason prefer plain Bonferroni Holm. Holm (Holm 1979) step-procedure controls FWER dependence structure. default pairwise_comparisons(). Tukey’s HSD (Tukey 1953; Hsu 1996) uses studentized range distribution give exact simultaneous FWER control complete set pairwise comparisons (exact equal replication; Tukey–Kramer extension covers unequal replication). exploits exact joint distribution pairwise differences, powerful Bonferroni Holm complete set — default multiple_comparisons(). Dunnett’s test (Dunnett 1955) exact FWER procedure specific family comparing every treatment single control. Like Tukey pairs, exploits known joint distribution comparisons (correlation induced shared control), powerful generic adjustment family. default reference_comparisons(). False discovery rate (FDR) expected proportion false rejections among rejected comparisons. less stringent FWER earns keep comparisons many somewhat exploratory, plant breeding. Benjamini–Hochberg ("BH"/"fdr") (Benjamini Hochberg 1995) controls FDR independence positive dependence (pairwise comparisons satisfy); "" valid arbitrary dependence conservative. natural question pairwise_comparisons() reference_comparisons() offer adjust = "tukey". Tukey’s critical value calibrated largest difference among means; applied chosen subset still controls FWER, sized comparisons making, overly conservative conceptually mismatched (Hsu 1996; Bretz et al. 2010). selected set, method calibrated set (Holm, Bonferroni, BH) preferred; -vs-control set, Dunnett calibrated choice. Conversely, complete set Tukey powerful uses known structure pairwise differences. title: Adding Orientation Arrows and Annotations to Field Layout Plots
text: presenting field trial layouts, ’s often important provide spatial context orientation arrows (indicating north directions) annotations reference physical features field (boundaries, gates, adjacent landmarks). vignette demonstrates add elements field layout plots generated biometryassist package. biometryassist package generates field layout plots ggplot2 objects, means can enhance using standard ggplot2 functions extensions. vignette show : Add orientation arrows arbitrary rotation Add text annotations outside plotting area label field features

title: Adding Orientation Arrows and Annotations to Field Layout Plots
text: First, let’s load required packages generate basic experimental design:  design() function creates field layout stores des.$plot.des ggplot2 object. can now enhance plot additional annotations.
code: library(biometryassist) library(ggplot2) library(ggspatial)  # Generate a completely randomized design (CRD) des.out <- design(   type = "crd",    treatments = c(1, 5, 10, 20),   reps = 5,    nrows = 4,    ncols = 5,    seed = 42 ) ## Source of Variation                     df ## ============================================= ## treatments                              3 ## Residual                                16 ## ============================================= ## Total                                   19

title: Adding Orientation Arrows and Annotations to Field Layout Plots
text: Orientation arrows help readers understand spatial layout field trial relative cardinal directions reference points. particularly useful field orientation affects environmental conditions like sun exposure prevailing winds. ggspatial package provides annotation_north_arrow() function, specifically designed adding directional arrows plots. ’s primarily intended spatial data coordinate reference systems, works well field layout plots .  Let’s break component code: annotation_north_arrow() arguments: location = "tr": Places arrow top-right corner. options include "tl" (top-left), "bl" (bottom-left), "br" (bottom-right), can specify exact coordinates numeric vector c(x, y). rotation = 53: Rotates arrow 53 degrees anti-clockwise north. allows indicate true field orientation. example, north field 45 degrees clockwise top plot, use rotation = 45. Use rotation = 0 north aligns top plot. pad_x = unit(-1.5, "cm"): Moves arrow horizontally. Negative values move right, positive values move left. allows fine-tuning arrow position. pad_y = unit(0.5, "cm"): Moves arrow vertically. Positive values move , negative values move . style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering(): Specifies visual style arrow. available styles include: north_arrow_minimal(): Simple arrow design north_arrow_nautical(): Compass-style design north_arrow_orienteering(): Classic orienteering map style Within style function, can customize: line_width: Thickness arrow lines text_size: Size “N” label Additional ggplot2 modifications: coord_cartesian(clip = ""): critical. default, ggplot2 clips (cuts ) elements fall outside plotting area. Setting clip = "" allows arrow drawn outside plot panel, necessary using padding position beyond plot boundaries. theme(plot.margin = ...): Expands margins around plot create space arrow. margin() function takes four values: top, right, bottom, left. Units specified unit parameter (, points). increase right margin 50pt accommodate arrow. can customize arrow style parameters match needs:
code: des.out$plot.des +    annotation_north_arrow(     location = "tr",     rotation = 53,     pad_x = unit(-1.5, "cm"),     pad_y = unit(0.5, "cm"),     style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering(       line_width = 1,       text_size = 10     )   ) +   coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +   theme(plot.margin = margin(t = 10, r = 50, b = 10, l = 10, unit = "pt")) des.out$plot.des +    annotation_north_arrow(     location = "tr",     rotation = 30,  # Different rotation angle     pad_x = unit(-1, "cm"),     pad_y = unit(0.3, "cm"),     style = north_arrow_minimal(  # Different style       line_width = 1.5,       text_size = 12     )   ) +   coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +   theme(plot.margin = margin(t = 10, r = 50, b = 10, l = 10, unit = "pt"))

title: Adding Orientation Arrows and Annotations to Field Layout Plots
text: addition orientation, ’s often helpful label physical features field site, boundaries, access points, adjacent landmarks. annotations provide practical context field operations help trial management. annotate() function allows us add text (geometric objects) specific locations plot. Unlike north arrow, annotations use plot’s coordinate system.  Let’s examine annotation detail: First annotation - “Boundary Road” (vertical text right): "text": Specifies ’re adding text annotation (options include "rect", "segment", etc.) x = 5.8: x-coordinate text. Since plot 5 columns (numbered 1-5), placing 5.8 positions outside right edge plot. y = mean(des.$design$row): Centers text vertically calculating mean row numbers. ensures text centered regardless number rows. label = "Boundary Road": text display. angle = 270: Rotates text 270 degrees (-90 degrees), making read top bottom. conventional labels right side plots. hjust = 0.5: Horizontal justification. 0.5 centers text horizontally relative x-coordinate. vjust = -0.5: Vertical justification. Negative values move text away plot (outward), positive values move toward plot. ensures text doesn’t overlap plot area. size = 6: font size text. Second annotation - “Gate” (horizontal text top): x = 1.5: Positions text column 1.5 (columns 1 2). y = 5: Places top row (row 4), outside plotting area. angle specified, text remains horizontal (0 degrees). parameters work similarly first annotation. Critical settings external annotations: xlim ylim explicitly set visible data range. essential add annotations coordinates outside data range (like x = 5.8), ggplot2 normally expand axis limits include points, stretching plot. explicitly setting limits match original plot grid (columns 0.5-5.5, rows 0.5-4.5), prevent expansion. clip = "" allows drawing outside limits, annotations x = 5.8 y = 5 still visible. theme(plot.margin = ...): Expands margins create physical space text. right margin set 70pt accommodate “Boundary Road”, top margin 20pt “Gate”. field layout plot uses row column numbers coordinates: - Columns numbered 1 ncols (, 1 5) - Rows numbered 1 nrows (, 1 4) - plot cell centered integer coordinates position annotations: - Outside right edge: Use x > max column number (e.g., x = 5.8 max column 5) - Outside top edge: Use y > max row number (e.g., y = 5 max row 4) - Outside left edge: Use x < min column number (e.g., x = 0.2 min column 1) - Outside bottom edge: Use y < min row number (e.g., y = 0.2 min row 1) can add many annotations needed chaining multiple annotate() calls:
code: des.out$plot.des +    annotate(     "text",     x = 5.8,     y = mean(des.out$design$row),     label = "Boundary Road",     angle = 270,     hjust = 0.5,     vjust = -0.5,     size = 6   ) +   annotate(     "text",     x = 1.5,     y = 5,     label = "Gate",     hjust = 0.5,     vjust = -0.5,     size = 6   ) +   coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0.5, 5.5), ylim = c(0.5, 4.5), clip = "off") +   theme(plot.margin = margin(t = 10, r = 70, b = 20, l = 10, unit = "pt"),         legend.position="none") des.out$plot.des +    annotate("text", x = 5.8, y = mean(des.out$design$row),             label = "Boundary Road", angle = 270, hjust = 0.5, vjust = -0.5, size = 6) +   annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = 5,             label = "Gate", hjust = 0.5, vjust = -0.5, size = 6) +   annotate("text", x = 0.2, y = mean(des.out$design$row),             label = "Irrigation Line", angle = 90, hjust = 0.5, vjust = -0.5, size = 5) +   annotate("text", x = mean(des.out$design$col), y = 0.2,             label = "Drainage Ditch", hjust = 0.5, vjust = 1, size = 5) +   coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0.5, 5.5), ylim = c(0.5, 4.5), clip = "off") +   theme(plot.margin = margin(t = 20, r = 70, b = 30, l = 50, unit = "pt"),         legend.position="none")

title: Adding Orientation Arrows and Annotations to Field Layout Plots
text: Solution: Increase relevant plot margin using theme(plot.margin = margin(...)). Ensure margins large enough accommodate text. Solution: using annotate() coordinates outside plot range, always specify explicit limits coord_cartesian(xlim = ..., ylim = ...) prevent automatic axis expansion. Solution: 1. Check coord_cartesian(clip = "") included 2. Verify plot margins large enough 3. Adjust pad_x pad_y values position arrow within margin area Solution: Adjust angle parameter: - right side labels reading upward: angle = 90 - right side labels reading downward (conventional): angle = 270 - left side labels: reverse angles

title: Context: Modifications to multiple_comparisons()
text: Critical correctness issue: current calculate_pvalue_matrix() uses ptukey therefore returns Tukey-adjusted p-values. Passing p.adjust() result double-adjustment. fix compute raw two-sided t-test p-value matrix first: branch: - adjust = "tukey": apply ptukey currently (preserves existing behaviour) - method: apply p.adjust(p_raw_vector, method = adjust) lower-triangle vector raw p-values
code: t_stat <- abs(pred_i - pred_j) / sed[i, j] p_raw  <- 2 * pt(-abs(t_stat), df = ndf)

text: Wrap comparison block split-apply-combine loop group levels: SED matrix get_predictions() always full n×n matrix across model types (asreml, lm, aovlist, lmerMod), submatrix slicing sed[idx, idx] safe correct supported model types.
code: by_vals     <- interaction(pp[, by, drop = FALSE], drop = TRUE) groups_list <- split(seq_len(nrow(pp)), by_vals)  results <- lapply(groups_list, function(idx) {   pp_g  <- pp[idx, , drop = FALSE]   sed_g <- sed[idx, idx, drop = FALSE]   # submatrix — correct for all model types   ndf_g <- if (is.matrix(ndf)) ndf[idx, idx, drop = FALSE] else ndf   # run calculate_raw_pvalue_matrix, get_diffs_from_pvalues,   # add_letter_groups etc. on pp_g / sed_g / ndf_g   # return annotated pp_g })  pp <- do.call(rbind, results)

title: Context: Modifications to multiple_comparisons()
text: Bonferroni / Holm: conservative valid dependence — familywise error rate guarantee holds BH / FDR: approximately valid positive dependence, pairwise comparisons satisfy Tukey: exact complete set pairwise comparisons; less appropriate selective comparisons pairs argument (selective comparisons), Bonferroni Holm just selected pairs statistically defensible Tukey P-value adjustment via p.adjust() assumes tests passed flat vector; use lower-triangle vector raw p-value matrix

title: Copilot Instructions
text: package centers two workflows: experimental design generation post-model analysis/visualisation. Exported functions listed NAMESPACE (auto-generated - hand-edit).

title: Copilot Instructions
text: R/design.R -> design() main entry point. dispatches type (crd, rcbd, lsd, split, strip, crossed:<base>) underlying agricolae design generators, enriches result. R/design_helpers.R - internal helpers (des_info, get_design_info, etc.) augment raw agricolae design object row/column layout. R/satab.R - builds skeletal ANOVA table (satab object, S3 class print method) describing design’s sources variation. R/create_buffers.R (add_buffers) - adds buffer plots (edge/rows/columns/blocks) design layout. R/export_excel.R (export_design_to_excel) - writes design styled workbook. design() returns list class design containing $design (data frame), $plot.des (ggplot/patchwork), $satab, optionally $seed. Plotting done via autoplot.design S3 method.

title: Copilot Instructions
text: functions operate already-fitted model objects (aov, asreml, lme/nlme, lmerMod/lme4, sommer, ARTool). package model-engine-agnostic via S3 dispatch Enhances/Suggests packages. R/mct.R (multiple_comparisons) - Tukey HSD / multiple comparison predicted means, letter groupings (multcompView), back-transformation support, confidence/comparison intervals. Returns mct S3 object (print autoplot methods). R/prediction_methods.R - get_predictions() generics extract predicted means supported model class; abstraction layer lets multiple_comparisons work across engines. R/resplot.R + R/resplot_methods.R (resplot/resplt) - diagnostic residual plots, dispatched per model class. R/heatmap.R (heat_map) - spatial heat maps trial data/residuals row/column. R/variogram.r (variogram) - spatial variograms ASReml-R models (see two reference PDFs repo root: Stefanova 2009, Gilmour 1997). R/summary_graph.R (summary_graph), R/logltest.R (logl_test) - additional analysis helpers. R/autoplot.R - S3 autoplot methods (autoplot.design, autoplot.mct) built ggplot2; patchwork composes multi-panel output.

title: Copilot Instructions
text: R/install_asreml.R (install_asreml, update_asreml) downloads installs correct ASReml-R binary user’s OS/R version shortlink. ASReml commercial CRAN, installation conveniences, dependencies.

title: Add buffers to an existing design — add_buffers
code: # Create a simple CRD design des <- design(type = "crd", treatments = c("A", "B"), reps = 3, nrows = 2, ncols = 3, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> treatments                              1 #> Residual                                4 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   5   # Plot the original design autoplot(des)   # Add edge buffers (a border around the whole field) and plot autoplot(add_buffers(des, type = "edge"))   # A buffer row between every pair of rows autoplot(add_buffers(des, type = "row"))   # Two buffer rows between every pair of rows autoplot(add_buffers(des, type = "double row"))   # For block designs, buffer the boundaries between blocks des_rcbd <- design(type = "rcbd", treatments = LETTERS[1:4], reps = 4,                    nrows = 4, ncols = 4, brows = 2, bcols = 2, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> Block stratum                           3 #> --------------------------------------------- #> treatments                              3 #> Residual                                9 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   15   # One buffer at each internal block boundary autoplot(add_buffers(des_rcbd, type = "block"))   # A full buffer ring surrounding each block autoplot(add_buffers(des_rcbd, type = "double block"))   # Use `by` to buffer where a grouping column changes. In a split-plot the # `plots` column identifies each wholeplot, so this rings every wholeplot: des_sp <- design(type = "split", treatments = c("A", "B"), sub_treatments = 1:4,                  reps = 4, nrows = 8, ncols = 4, brows = 4, bcols = 2, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                          df #> ================================================== #> Block stratum                                3 #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Whole plot stratum #>          treatments                          1 #> Whole plot Residual                          3 #> ================================================== #> Subplot stratum #>          sub_treatments                      3 #>          treatments:sub_treatments           3 #>          Subplot Residual                   18 #> ================================================== #> Total                                       31  autoplot(add_buffers(des_sp, type = "double block", by = "plots"))   # ... or just a buffer row between the wholeplot bands autoplot(add_buffers(des_sp, type = "row", by = "plots"))

title: Plot the predicted means from a multiple comparisons test — autoplot.mct
text: object mct object, produced multiple_comparisons(). size Increase decrease text size within plot treatment labels. Numeric default value 4. label_height Height text labels upper error bar plot. Default 0.1 (10%) difference upper lower error bars top error bar. Values > 1 interpreted actual value upper error bar. rotation Rotate x axis labels treatment group labels within plot. Allows easier reading long axis treatment labels. Number 0 360 (inclusive) - default 0 axis_rotation Enables rotation x axis independently group labels within plot. label_rotation Enables rotation treatment group labels independently x axis labels within plot. type string specifying type plot display. One "point" (default; point estimates), "line" (point estimates joined line), "column" (also "col" "bar"; column graph). Error bars added according errorbar_type unless include_errorbar = FALSE. errorbar_type string (default "ci") specifying error bars represent. "ci" draws interval around mean (interval type chosen via int.type multiple_comparisons()). "hsd" draws single Tukey's Honest Significant Difference reference bar instead per-mean intervals. title: Create a complete experimental design with graph of design layout and skeletal ANOVA table — design
code: # Completely Randomised Design des.out <- design(type = "crd", treatments = c(1, 5, 10, 20),                   reps = 5, nrows = 4, ncols = 5, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> treatments                              3 #> Residual                                16 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   19   # Randomised Complete Block Design des.out <- design("rcbd", treatments = LETTERS[1:11], reps = 4,                   nrows = 11, ncols = 4, brows = 11, bcols = 1, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> Block stratum                           3 #> --------------------------------------------- #> treatments                              10 #> Residual                                30 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   43   # Latin Square Design # Doesn't require reps argument des.out <- design(type = "lsd", c("S1", "S2", "S3", "S4"),                   nrows = 4, ncols = 4, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> Row                                     3 #> Column                                  3 #> treatments                              3 #> Residual                                6 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   15   # Factorial Design (Crossed, Completely Randomised) des.out <- design(type = "crossed:crd", treatments = c(3, 2),                   reps = 3, nrows = 6, ncols = 3, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> A                                       2 #> B                                       1 #> A:B                                     2 #> Residual                                12 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   17   # Factorial Design (Crossed, Completely Randomised), renaming factors des.out <- design(type = "crossed:crd", treatments = c(3, 2),                   reps = 3, nrows = 6, ncols = 3, seed = 42,                   fac.names = list(N = c(50, 100, 150),                                    Water = c("Irrigated", "Rain-fed"))) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> N                                       2 #> Water                                   1 #> N:Water                                 2 #> Residual                                12 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   17   # Factorial Design (Crossed, Randomised Complete Block Design), # changing separation between factors des.out <- design(type = "crossed:rcbd", treatments = c(3, 2),                   reps = 3, nrows = 6, ncols = 3,                   brows = 6, bcols = 1,                   seed = 42, fac.sep = c(":", "_")) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> Block stratum                           2 #> --------------------------------------------- #> A                                       2 #> B                                       1 #> A:B                                     2 #> Residual                                10 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   17   # Factorial Design (Nested, Latin Square) trt <- c("A1", "A2", "A3", "A4", "B1", "B2", "B3") des.out <- design(type = "lsd", treatments = trt,                   nrows = 7, ncols = 7, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                     df #> ============================================= #> Row                                     6 #> Column                                  6 #> treatments                              6 #> Residual                                30 #> ============================================= #> Total                                   48   # Split plot design des.out <- design(type = "split", treatments = c("A", "B"), sub_treatments = 1:4,                   reps = 4, nrows = 8, ncols = 4, brows = 4, bcols = 2, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                          df #> ================================================== #> Block stratum                                3 #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Whole plot stratum #>          treatments                          1 #> Whole plot Residual                          3 #> ================================================== #> Subplot stratum #>          sub_treatments                      3 #>          treatments:sub_treatments           3 #>          Subplot Residual                   18 #> ================================================== #> Total                                       31   # Alternative arrangement of the same design as above des.out <- design(type = "split", treatments = c("A", "B"), sub_treatments = 1:4,                   reps = 4, nrows = 8, ncols = 4, brows = 4, bcols = 2,                   byrow = FALSE, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                          df #> ================================================== #> Block stratum                                3 #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Whole plot stratum #>          treatments                          1 #> Whole plot Residual                          3 #> ================================================== #> Subplot stratum #>          sub_treatments                      3 #>          treatments:sub_treatments           3 #>          Subplot Residual                   18 #> ================================================== #> Total                                       31   # Strip plot design des.out <- design(type = "strip", treatments = c("A", "B", "C"), sub_treatments = 1:4,                   reps = 4, nrows = 12, ncols = 4, brows = 3, bcols = 4, seed = 42) #> Source of Variation                          df #> ================================================== #> Block stratum                                3 #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Row strip stratum #>          treatments                          2 #> treatments Residual                          6 #> ================================================== #> Column strip stratum #>          sub_treatments                      3 #> sub_treatments Residual                      9 #> ================================================== #> Observational unit stratum #>          treatments:sub_treatments           6 #> Interaction Residual                        18 #> ================================================== #> Total                                       47

title: Perform Multiple Comparison Tests on a statistical model — multiple_comparisons
code: # Fit aov model model <- aov(Petal.Length ~ Species, data = iris)  # Display the ANOVA table for the model anova(model) #> Analysis of Variance Table #>  #> Response: Petal.Length #>            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)     #> Species     2 437.10 218.551  1180.2 < 2.2e-16 *** #> Residuals 147  27.22   0.185                       #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1  # Determine ranking and groups according to Tukey's Test (with Tukey intervals) pred.out <- multiple_comparisons(model, classify = "Species")  # Display the predicted values table pred.out #> Multiple Comparisons of Means: Tukey's HSD Test #> Significance level: 0.05  #> HSD value: 0.20378  #>  #> Predicted values: #>      Species predicted.value std.error  df groups   ci  low   up #> 1     setosa            1.46      0.06 147      a 0.12 1.34 1.58 #> 2 versicolor            4.26      0.06 147      b 0.12 4.14 4.38 #> 3  virginica            5.55      0.06 147      c 0.12 5.43 5.67  # Access the p-value matrix pred.out$pairwise_pvalues #>                  setosa   versicolor    virginica #> setosa     1.000000e+00 2.997602e-15 2.997602e-15 #> versicolor 2.997602e-15 1.000000e+00 2.997602e-15 #> virginica  2.997602e-15 2.997602e-15 1.000000e+00  # Access the HSD value pred.out$hsd #> [1] 0.20378  # Show the predicted values plot autoplot(pred.out, label_height = 0.5)   # Use traditional confidence intervals instead of Tukey comparison intervals pred.out.ci <- multiple_comparisons(model, classify = "Species", int.type = "ci") pred.out.ci #> Multiple Comparisons of Means: Tukey's HSD Test #> Significance level: 0.05  #> HSD value: 0.20378  #>  #> Predicted values: #>      Species predicted.value std.error  df groups   ci  low   up #> 1     setosa            1.46      0.06 147      a 0.12 1.34 1.58 #> 2 versicolor            4.26      0.06 147      b 0.12 4.14 4.38 #> 3  virginica            5.55      0.06 147      c 0.12 5.43 5.67  # Plot without confidence intervals pred.out.none <- multiple_comparisons(model, classify = "Species", int.type = "none") autoplot(pred.out.none)   # Use a different p-value adjustment instead of Tukey's HSD multiple_comparisons(model, classify = "Species", adjust = "fdr") #> Multiple Comparisons of Means: p-value adjustment = fdr  #> Significance level: 0.05  #>  #> Predicted values: #>      Species predicted.value std.error  df groups   ci  low   up #> 1     setosa            1.46      0.06 147      a 0.12 1.34 1.58 #> 2 versicolor            4.26      0.06 147      b 0.12 4.14 4.38 #> 3  virginica            5.55      0.06 147      c 0.12 5.43 5.67  # `by`: run the comparisons independently within each level of another factor. # Here a 2 x 3 factorial - compare tension levels within each wool type. m_wb <- aov(breaks ~ wool * tension, data = warpbreaks) mc_by <- multiple_comparisons(m_wb, classify = "wool:tension", by = "wool") mc_by #> Multiple Comparisons of Means: Tukey's HSD Test #> Significance level: 0.05  #> HSD value: varies by comparison (see $hsd) #>   #>  #> Predicted values: #>   wool tension predicted.value std.error df groups   ci   low    up #> 1    A       M           24.00      3.65 48      a 7.33 16.67 31.33 #> 2    A       H           24.56      3.65 48      a 7.33 17.22 31.89 #> 3    A       L           44.56      3.65 48      b 7.33 37.22 51.89 #> 4    B       H           18.78      3.65 48      a 7.33 11.45 26.11 #> 5    B       L           28.22      3.65 48      a 7.33 20.89 35.55 #> 6    B       M           28.78      3.65 48      a 7.33 21.45 36.11 autoplot(mc_by) # faceted by wool   # AOV model example with transformation my_iris <- iris my_iris$Petal.Length <- exp(my_iris$Petal.Length) # Create exponential response exp_model <- aov(Petal.Length ~ Species, data = my_iris)  resplot(exp_model) # Residual plot shows problems   # Fit a new model using a log transformation of the response log_model <- aov(log(Petal.Length) ~ Species, data = my_iris)  resplot(log_model) # Looks much better   # Display the ANOVA table for the model anova(log_model) #> Analysis of Variance Table #>  #> Response: log(Petal.Length) #>            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)     #> Species     2 437.10 218.551  1180.2 < 2.2e-16 *** #> Residuals 147  27.22   0.185                       #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1  # Back transform, because the "original" data was exponential pred.out <- multiple_comparisons(log_model, classify = "Species",                                    trans = "log") #> Warning: Offset value assumed to be 0. Change with `offset` argument.  # Display the predicted values table pred.out #> Multiple Comparisons of Means: Tukey's HSD Test #> Significance level: 0.05  #> HSD value: 0.20378  #>  #> Predicted values: #>      Species predicted.value std.error  df groups   ci PredictedValue ApproxSE #> 1     setosa            1.46      0.06 147      a 0.12           4.31     0.26 #> 2 versicolor            4.26      0.06 147      b 0.12          70.81     4.31 #> 3  virginica            5.55      0.06 147      c 0.12         257.75    15.69 #>      low     up #> 1   3.83   4.87 #> 2  62.79  79.86 #> 3 228.54 290.69  # Show the predicted values plot autoplot(pred.out, label_height = 15)   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # ASReml-R Example library(asreml)  # Fit ASReml-R model model.asr <- asreml(yield ~ Nitrogen + Variety + Nitrogen:Variety,                     random = ~ Blocks + Blocks:Wplots,                     residual = ~ units,                     data = asreml::oats)  wald(model.asr) # Nitrogen main effect significant  #Determine ranking and groups according to Tukey's Test pred.out <- multiple_comparisons(model.obj = model.asr, classify = "Nitrogen",                     descending = TRUE)  print(pred.out, decimals = 5)  # Example using a box-cox transformation set.seed(42) # See the seed for reproducibility resp <- rnorm(n = 50, 5, 1)^3 trt <- as.factor(sample(rep(LETTERS[1:10], 5), 50)) block <- as.factor(rep(1:5, each = 10)) ex_data <- data.frame(resp, trt, block)  # Change one treatment random values to get significant difference ex_data$resp[ex_data$trt=="A"] <- rnorm(n = 5, 7, 1)^3  model.asr <- asreml(resp ~ trt,                     random = ~ block,                     residual = ~ units,                     data = ex_data)  resplot(model.asr)  # lambda = 1/3 from MASS::boxcox() model.asr <- asreml(resp^(1/3) ~ trt,                     random = ~ block,                     residual = ~ units,                     data = ex_data)  resplot(model.asr) # Look much better  pred.out <- multiple_comparisons(model.obj = model.asr,                                  classify = "trt",                                  trans = "power", power = (1/3))  pred.out autoplot(pred.out, label_height = 0.5) } # }

versicolor    -1.08        0.25        1.33 #> 2     setosa  virginica     setosa - virginica    -1.78        0.25        2.03 #> 3 versicolor  virginica versicolor - virginica    -0.70        1.33        2.03 #>   std.error statistic  df  p.value conf.low conf.high #> 1      0.04    -26.39 147 2.51e-57    -1.16     -1.00 #> 2      0.04    -43.49 147 2.39e-85    -1.86     -1.70 #> 3      0.04    -17.10 147 8.82e-37    -0.78     -0.62  # A selected subset pairwise_comparisons(     dat.aov,     classify = "Species",     pairs = c("setosa-versicolor", "setosa-virginica") ) #> Pairwise comparisons of means #> Classify: Species  #> Adjustment method: holm  #> Significance level: 0.05  #>  #>   level1     level2          comparison estimate level1.mean level2.mean #> 1 setosa versicolor setosa - versicolor    -1.08        0.25        1.33 #> 2 setosa  virginica  setosa - virginica    -1.78        0.25        2.03 #>   std.error statistic  df  p.value conf.low conf.high #> 1      0.04    -26.39 147 1.25e-57    -1.16      -1.0 #> 2      0.04    -43.49 147 1.59e-85    -1.86      -1.7  # A general (non-pairwise) contrast: setosa vs the average of the others pairwise_comparisons(     dat.aov,     classify = "Species",     contrasts = list(         "setosa vs rest" = c(setosa = 1, versicolor = -0.5, virginica = -0.5)     ) ) #> Contrasts of means #> Classify: Species  #> Adjustment method: holm  #> Significance level: 0.05  #>  #>       comparison estimate std.error statistic  df  p.value conf.low conf.high #> 1 setosa vs rest    -1.43      0.04    -40.34 147 2.12e-81     -1.5     -1.36  # `by`: the same comparisons, adjusted independently within each group. code: # Means plot of each level vs the reference (significant ones marked with *) dat.aov <- aov(Petal.Width ~ Species, data = iris) rc <- reference_comparisons(dat.aov, classify = "Species", reference = "setosa") autoplot(rc)  dat.aov <- aov(weight ~ feed, data = chickwts)  # Compare every feed against the "casein" control (exact Dunnett) reference_comparisons(dat.aov, classify = "feed", reference = "casein") #> Comparisons against a reference level #> Classify: feed  #> Reference: casein  #> Adjustment method: dunnett  #> Significance level: 0.05  #>  #>      level1 level2         comparison estimate level1.mean level2.mean #> 1 horsebean casein horsebean - casein  -163.38      160.20      323.58 #> 2   linseed casein   linseed - casein  -104.83      218.75      323.58 #> 3  meatmeal casein  meatmeal - casein   -46.67      276.91      323.58 #> 4   soybean casein   soybean - casein   -77.15      246.43      323.58 #> 5 sunflower casein sunflower - casein     5.33      328.92      323.58 #>   std.error statistic df  p.value conf.low conf.high #> 1     23.49     -6.96 65 7.56e-09  -223.93   -102.83 #> 2     22.39     -4.68 65 6.32e-05  -162.57    -47.10 #> 3     22.90     -2.04 65 1.67e-01  -105.70     12.36 #> 4     21.58     -3.58 65 3.00e-03  -132.79    -21.52 #> 5     22.39      0.24 65 9.99e-01   -52.40     63.07  # A different adjustment can be requested (a message notes that Dunnett is the # exact option for this family): reference_comparisons(     dat.aov,     classify = "feed",     reference = "casein",     adjust = "holm" ) #> Using adjust = "holm". For exact simultaneous control of all-vs-reference comparisons, adjust = "dunnett" is the exact method. #> Note: confidence intervals are per-comparison (not adjusted for multiplicity), while the p-values are adjusted. A comparison's interval can therefore exclude zero when its adjusted p-value is not significant at `sig` (or, less often, the reverse). #> Comparisons against a reference level #> Classify: feed  #> Reference: casein  #> Adjustment method: holm  #> Significance level: 0.05  #>  #>      level1 level2         comparison estimate level1.mean level2.mean #> 1 horsebean casein horsebean - casein  -163.38      160.20      323.58 #> 2   linseed casein   linseed - casein  -104.83      218.75      323.58 #> 3  meatmeal casein  meatmeal - casein   -46.67      276.91      323.58 #> 4   soybean casein   soybean - casein   -77.15      246.43      323.58 #> 5 sunflower casein sunflower - casein     5.33      328.92      323.58 #>   std.error statistic df  p.value conf.low conf.high #> 1     23.49     -6.96 65 1.03e-08  -210.29   -116.48 #> 2     22.39     -4.68 65 5.97e-05  -149.55    -60.11 #> 3     22.90     -2.04 65 9.11e-02   -92.40     -0.95 #> 4     21.58     -3.58 65 2.00e-03  -120.25    -34.06 #> 5     22.39      0.24 65 8.12e-01   -39.39     50.05  # `by`: compare each level against the reference *within* each group, adjusted # independently. Here a 2 x 3 factorial - each tension level vs the "L" # reference within each wool type; autoplot() facets by wool. m_wb <- aov(breaks ~ wool * tension, data = warpbreaks) rc_by <- reference_comparisons(     m_wb,     classify = "wool:tension",     reference = "L",     by = "wool" ) rc_by #> Comparisons against a reference level #> Classify: wool:tension  #> Reference: L  #> Adjustment method: dunnett  #> Significance level: 0.05  #>  #>   wool level1 level2 comparison estimate level1.mean level2.mean std.error #> 1    A      M      L      M - L   -20.56       24.00       44.56      5.16 #> 2    A      H      L      H - L   -20.00       24.56       44.56      5.16 #> 3    B      M      L      M - L     0.56       28.78       28.22      5.16 #> 4    B      H      L      H - L    -9.44       18.78       28.22      5.16 #>   statistic df  p.value conf.low conf.high #> 1     -3.99 48 0.000447   -32.31     -8.80 #> 2     -3.88 48 0.000626   -31.75     -8.25 #> 3      0.11 48 0.992000   -11.20     12.31 #> 4     -1.83 48 0.130000   -21.20      2.31 autoplot(rc_by)

title: biometryassist 1.5.0
text: multiple_comparisons(): plot, label_height, rotation, save, savename arguments now deprecated removed future version. Use autoplot(<result>) plotting (pass label_height rotation ), write.csv(result$predictions, "file.csv") saving. pred, order, pred.obj arguments deprecated 1.1.0 earlier now removed. multiple_comparisons() gains adjust argument choose p-value adjustment method (stats::p.adjust() method, addition default Tukey’s HSD), argument run comparisons independently within groups. Added support aovlist nlme::lme() models multiple_comparisons() (#107). comparison functions now support afex (afex_aov), glmmTMB, sommer’s mmes() models. glmmTMB predictions link scale asymptotic degrees freedom (supply trans back-transform); sommer mmes() predictions SEDs come sommer’s native predict(), also asymptotic degrees freedom. resplot() now supports multi-stratum aov models fitted Error() (aovlist, one panel per error stratum), afex_aov models, Gaussian-family glmmTMB models. lme4breeding::lmebreed() (relationship-based mixed) models supported workflows: carry class lmerMod handled existing lmerMod methods. Comparisons correctly reflect relationship structure (validated ASReml-R), using Kenward-Roger degrees freedom. Deliberate boundaries (informative errors): ARTool (art) sommer’s legacy mmer models available comparison functions (ART uses aligned ranks — use ARTool::art.con(); refit mmer sommer::mmes()), though remain supported resplot(). Non-Gaussian glmmTMB models valid resplot()’s normal-Q-Q diagnostic point DHARMa::simulateResiduals() instead. New function pairwise_comparisons() test selected pairwise differences (general linear contrasts) predicted means, returning tidy table estimates, predicted means multiplicity-adjusted p-values, forest plot via autoplot(). New function reference_comparisons() compare every level single reference (control) level, using exact Dunnett test default. returns means-centric table (level’s mean, reference mean adjusted difference) means plot via autoplot().

title: biometryassist 1.5.0
text: Added ability add buffers double buffers around blocks. (#169) autoplot() multiple_comparisons() output gains several new options (thanks Michael Mumford, #161): type = "line" joins means line; include_errorbar include_lettering toggle error bars significance letters; errorbar_type = "hsd" draws single Tukey’s HSD reference bar instead per-mean intervals; trans_scale = TRUE plots back-transformed means model (transformed) scale exact back-transformed secondary axis. Added vignette, “Choosing interpreting multiple comparisons”, introducing pairwise_comparisons() reference_comparisons() alongside multiple_comparisons() giving guidance choosing multiplicity adjustment.

(#170) export_design_to_excel() function now less fragile. can handle design objects data frames input, alternative names row column columns.
